questions.text: What encryption algorithm does Ansible Vault use?
questions.explanation: Ansible Vault uses AES-256 (Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit keys) in CTR mode with HMAC-SHA256 authentication. The vault password is processed through PBKDF2 key derivation. This is symmetric encryption — the same password encrypts and decrypts.

questions.text: What YAML tag indicates an inline vault-encrypted variable?
questions.explanation: The !vault YAML tag marks a variable's value as vault-encrypted inline. When Ansible processes the variable, it automatically decrypts the value using the provided vault password. This allows mixing encrypted and plain variables in the same file.

questions.text: Which command encrypts a single string value for use as an inline variable?
questions.explanation: ansible-vault encrypt_string 'secret_value' --name 'var_name' outputs the encrypted variable block with the !vault YAML tag, ready to paste into a vars file or group_vars. Use --vault-id to specify which vault password to use.

questions.text: What does 'no_log: true' do in an Ansible task?

options.text: Disables fact gathering for that task
Prevents the task's arguments and output from appearing in logs
Runs the task without writing to the Ansible log file
Suppresses changed/ok status in play recap

questions.explanation: no_log: true prevents Ansible from printing the task's module arguments and return values in output or log files. Critical for tasks that use passwords or private keys. The task still runs and its status (ok/changed/failed) is shown, but sensitive values are redacted.

questions.text: How do you run a playbook with a vault password from a file?
questions.explanation: The --vault-password-file flag provides a path to a file containing the vault password (or a script that outputs it). You can also set ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE environment variable. The file should have no trailing newline and be mode 0600.

questions.text: What is the purpose of Vault IDs?

options.text: They provide unique identifiers for each encrypted file
They allow labeling encrypted content to use different passwords per environment
They are unique IDs generated by Ansible for tracking
They replace the vault password for RSA-based authentication

questions.explanation: Vault IDs (introduced in Ansible 2.4) let you label encrypted content with an identifier (e.g., dev, prod, staging) and associate each ID with a different password. This enables separate secrets for different environments while running a single playbook with multiple vault passwords.

questions.text: What is the recommended file structure for separating vault variables from plain variables?

options.text: Keep all variables in group_vars/all.yml and prefix secrets with vault_
Use group_vars/groupname/vars.yml for plain vars and group_vars/groupname/vault.yml for encrypted

questions.explanation: Best practice is to split group_vars into vars.yml (plain) and vault.yml (encrypted). Plain vars reference vault vars: db_password: '{{ vault_db_password }}'. This keeps diffs readable (plain vars show in git) while secrets stay encrypted. This is the most common enterprise pattern.

questions.text: Which command changes the vault password on an encrypted file?
questions.explanation: ansible-vault rekey decrypts the file with the current password and re-encrypts it with a new password. You must provide both old (--vault-password-file or interactive) and new passwords. This is used for password rotation without changing the encrypted content.

questions.text: When should you use 'ansible-vault view' vs 'ansible-vault decrypt'?

options.text: Use view to display content without modifying the file; use decrypt to save the plaintext to disk
Use view for strings; use decrypt for whole files
view is for YAML files; decrypt is for binary files

questions.explanation: ansible-vault view shows the decrypted content on-screen without writing plaintext to disk (safer). ansible-vault decrypt overwrites the file with plaintext, which is only appropriate temporarily (e.g., for editing with tools that don't support vault). Always re-encrypt after decrypting.

questions.text: Can you have both encrypted and unencrypted variables in the same YAML file?

options.text: No — the entire file must be encrypted or unencrypted
Yes — use !vault tag for specific variables while others remain plain
Yes — but only if the file starts with $ANSIBLE_VAULT
No — you must use separate files for vault and plain variables

questions.explanation: Yes, you can mix vault-encrypted and plain variables in one file. Encrypt only specific values using ansible-vault encrypt_string and the !vault tag. The rest of the file stays in plaintext. This is useful but the separate files pattern (vars.yml + vault.yml) is often cleaner for auditing.
